course.title: Cosplay Wig Styling
course.description: A self-contained 90-day tracker for five-minute cosplay wig styling micro-lessons.

lessons.title: What This Course Is
lessons.concept: This course is now built from micro-lessons. Each day teaches one small decision, word, or action instead of sending you into large tutorial sites.

glossary.definition: A short note replacing outside reading unless a lesson explicitly says otherwise.
The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.

lessons.practice: Read the lesson card and say what today's one tiny job is.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: The lesson feels like a five-minute task, not homework.
lessons.readiness: You can tell whether a future lesson is asking too much.

lessons.title: Wig Vocabulary Starts Small
lessons.objective: Learn the base words without memorizing a whole field.
lessons.concept: A few shared words make later lessons less confusing. Today only separates the wig base from the hair sewn onto it.

glossary.definition: The base of the wig that sits on your head and holds the hair or wefts.
A sewn strip of wig hair that can be added, removed, moved, or harvested.

lessons.practice: Point to where the cap is and where a weft would sit on any wig photo or wig you own.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which word describes the base, and which describes a strip of hair?
lessons.readiness: You can use cap and weft correctly in one sentence.

lessons.title: Fiber And Density
lessons.objective: Understand what the hair is and how full it is.
lessons.concept: Synthetic wig hair is fiber. Density is how much fiber is packed into an area, which changes fullness, weight, and cap coverage.

glossary.definition: The synthetic hair material the wig is made from.
How much hair is present in an area; higher density looks fuller but adds weight.

lessons.practice: Look at one wig image and label it low, medium, or high density by guess only.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Would more density help this wig, or make it heavier than needed?
lessons.readiness: You can explain density without touching styling tools.

lessons.title: Hairline And Lace Front
lessons.objective: Separate visible edge needs from the rest of the wig.
lessons.concept: A visible forehead or pulled-back style often needs better edge planning. Lace fronts are one solution, not the default answer for every wig.

glossary.definition: The visible edge where wig hair begins around the forehead, temples, sideburns, or nape.
A wig with sheer lace at the front where individual hairs create a realistic hairline.

lessons.practice: Pick one character and decide whether the front hairline is visible or hidden by bangs.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Does this character need realism at the edge, or just clean coverage?
lessons.readiness: You can say whether lace is useful for one character.

lessons.title: Soft, Supported, Or Helmet
lessons.objective: Sort hairstyles by structure before styling.
lessons.concept: Some wigs are mostly hair. Some need hidden support. Some are closer to a helmet covered in hair.

glossary.definition: Structure under the visible surface that creates lift, shape, strength, or balance.
A wig built over a rigid or semi-rigid shell, usually for large sculptural hair.

lessons.practice: Sort three character hairstyles into soft, supported, or helmet candidates.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which one stops behaving like hair?
lessons.readiness: You can choose a broad build category.

lessons.title: Make A Wig Map
lessons.objective: Turn a scary style into labeled zones.
lessons.concept: A wig map labels the visible hair, hidden support, hairline, attachments, and problem areas. It is a planning sketch, not art.

glossary.definition: A simple plan that labels what each part of the wig must do.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Draw a messy outline of one hairstyle and label three zones.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What part must be hidden for the style to work?
lessons.readiness: You have a labeled map with at least three zones.

lessons.title: Choose One Reference Angle
lessons.concept: A five-minute lesson can only use one decision. Start with the front view because it usually controls character recognition.

glossary.definition: How quickly the hairstyle is recognizable as the intended character.
The outer shape of the hairstyle, usually the first thing viewers recognize.

lessons.practice: Find or imagine one front-view reference and name the biggest shape.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What makes the character recognizable first?
lessons.readiness: You can name the main silhouette in one phrase.

lessons.title: Side View Check
lessons.concept: The side view reveals whether hair falls naturally, sticks out, or needs support. This prevents flat front-only planning.

glossary.definition: The empty shapes around hair chunks that help the silhouette read clearly.
The route weight and force take through the wig into anchors and the wearer's head.

lessons.practice: Look at a side view and mark one part that sticks out or floats.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where would gravity pull this shape?
lessons.readiness: You can name one likely support problem.

lessons.title: Back View Check
lessons.objective: Account for the view people forget.
lessons.concept: The back can expose wefts, gaps, ponytail anchors, and unfinished structure. A simple back-view note prevents surprise problems.

glossary.definition: Visible wig cap or weft tracks showing through the hair.
The direction fibers travel so the style, seams, and coverage look intentional.

lessons.practice: Write one sentence about what the back of the wig must show or hide.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would look unfinished from behind?
lessons.readiness: You can state one back-view requirement.

lessons.title: Tiny Materials List
lessons.objective: List only what the next task needs.
lessons.concept: A materials list should prevent friction, not become a shopping spiral. For today, only list the next three useful things.

glossary.definition: A short note replacing outside reading unless a lesson explicitly says otherwise.
Products and tools packed for quick fixes during wear.

lessons.practice: Write three items you would want nearby for a basic wig practice session.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What item would stop you from starting?
lessons.readiness: You have a three-item list.

lessons.title: Fit Comes First
lessons.concept: A styled wig that slips, pinches, or sits wrong will not be saved by better surface work. Fit is the foundation.

glossary.definition: How the wig or shell sits on the wearer's head without sliding, squeezing, or rubbing.
A spot where weight, edge, clip, strap, or shell presses uncomfortably.

lessons.practice: Put on or imagine a wig and name one place it might squeeze, slide, or rub.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where would you notice discomfort first?
lessons.readiness: You can name one fit risk.

lessons.title: Block Basics
lessons.concept: The block is the head form used for styling and building. If it is the wrong size, the finished wig can inherit that mistake.

glossary.definition: The head form used for styling, cutting, fitting, and building wig structures.
How the wig or shell sits on the wearer's head without sliding, squeezing, or rubbing.

lessons.practice: Write whether your work head is smaller, larger, or unknown compared with your head.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What error could that create?
lessons.readiness: You know whether your block needs checking.

lessons.title: Mark The Front
lessons.concept: A front mark, center line, and ear landmarks make later styling less guessy. This is especially useful before cutting.

glossary.definition: The visible edge where wig hair begins around the forehead, temples, sideburns, or nape.
The side tab area of a wig near the ear, important for fit and edge blending.

lessons.practice: On paper or a photo, mark front center and both ear areas.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would look wrong if the wig rotated slightly?
lessons.readiness: You can identify front center and ear zones.

lessons.title: Comfort Gap
lessons.concept: Rigid or bulky builds need room for hair, pins, padding, and movement. No style is done if it only fits a foam head.

glossary.definition: A spot where weight, edge, clip, strap, or shell presses uncomfortably.
A wig built over a rigid or semi-rigid shell, usually for large sculptural hair.

lessons.practice: Name one place a shell or heavy wig needs extra room.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would hurt after ten minutes?
lessons.readiness: You can name one comfort allowance.

lessons.title: Quick Wear Test Idea
lessons.concept: A wear test can be short. The point is to catch heat, slipping, pressure, or blocked vision before the wig is final.

glossary.definition: A timed trial wear to check heat, pressure, slipping, visibility, comfort, and failure points.
How the wig or shell sits on the wearer's head without sliding, squeezing, or rubbing.

lessons.practice: Write a 5-minute wear-test checklist with three things to notice.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would make you stop wearing it?
lessons.readiness: You have a tiny wear-test checklist.

lessons.title: Synthetic Fiber Rule
lessons.objective: Learn the heat warning before heat work.
lessons.concept: Heat-resistant fiber can still melt. Always test a hidden strand or scrap before trusting a temperature.

glossary.definition: Able to tolerate controlled heat better than standard synthetic fiber, but still meltable.
A hidden test on a small fiber section before using heat, dye, or product broadly.

lessons.practice: Write the sentence: test hidden fiber before visible heat.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What happens if the label is wrong?
lessons.readiness: You know the test comes before the tool.

lessons.title: Cool-Down Rule
lessons.concept: Synthetic fiber often holds the shape it cools in. Moving it too soon can weaken the set.

glossary.definition: The period after heat shaping when synthetic fiber locks into its new shape.
Synthetic fiber's tendency to keep the shape it cooled or was stored in.

lessons.practice: Name one shape that would need to cool while held in place.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Why is waiting part of the technique?
lessons.readiness: You can explain heat, hold, cool.

lessons.title: Tension Rule
lessons.concept: Tension helps straightening, setting, and smoothing, but too much pull can stretch, frizz, or deform fiber.

glossary.definition: Controlled pulling force used while cutting, straightening, setting, or anchoring hair.
Shaping synthetic fiber with heat, steam, tension, clips, or rollers until it cools.

lessons.practice: Mime or imagine holding a small section straight while it cools.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where is the line between controlled and rough?
lessons.readiness: You can describe gentle tension.

lessons.title: Steam As Moist Heat
lessons.concept: Steam can relax or reshape synthetic fiber with less direct contact than a hot iron, but it is still heat.

glossary.definition: Shaping synthetic fiber with heat, steam, tension, clips, or rollers until it cools.
Synthetic fiber's tendency to keep the shape it cooled or was stored in.

lessons.practice: Write one job steam might do: relax, smooth, reshape, or reset.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Why is steam not automatically safe?
lessons.readiness: You can name one steam use and one caution.

lessons.title: Heat Log
lessons.concept: A heat log records tool, temperature, time, and result. This keeps successful tests from becoming guesses.

glossary.definition: A hidden test on a small fiber section before using heat, dye, or product broadly.
The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.

lessons.practice: Create a four-field heat log template.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What detail would you forget later?
lessons.readiness: You have a reusable heat-test note.

lessons.title: Detangle Ends First
lessons.concept: Detangling from the ends upward keeps knots from tightening into the wig. Long wigs especially need patience here.

glossary.definition: Removing knots gently, usually from ends upward, to avoid tightening tangles.
Frizz caused by rubbing against clothes, hands, collars, bags, or storage surfaces.

lessons.practice: Write the order: ends, middle, roots.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What causes frizz besides tangles?
lessons.readiness: You know the detangling direction.

lessons.title: Storage Shape
lessons.concept: Storage should protect the shape you built. A loose wig and a spiked wig should not be packed the same way.

glossary.definition: The outer shape of the hairstyle, usually the first thing viewers recognize.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Name one storage risk for a long wig and one for a styled wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would get crushed first?
lessons.readiness: You can choose storage based on shape.

lessons.title: Repair Kit Tiny Version
lessons.concept: A repair kit should match the wig. Do not pack everything; pack for the most likely failure.

glossary.definition: Products and tools packed for quick fixes during wear.
A timed trial wear to check heat, pressure, slipping, visibility, comfort, and failure points.

lessons.practice: List three repair items for a simple styled wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which failure is most likely?
lessons.readiness: You have a tiny repair-kit draft.

lessons.title: Cleaning Caution
lessons.objective: Know when not to wash aggressively.
lessons.concept: Lace, product, glued pieces, and structured shapes can be damaged by rough cleaning. Cleaning is a plan, not a panic move.

glossary.definition: A visible boundary such as hairline, lace, sideburn, nape, shell rim, or seam.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Name one part of a styled wig you would clean gently.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What could loosen or frizz?
lessons.readiness: You can name one cleaning risk.

lessons.title: Maintenance Habit
lessons.concept: A five-minute end-of-session habit saves future work: remove loose pins, note damage, protect shape, and store cleanly.

glossary.definition: A quick prompt that turns a task into a usable decision or lesson.
The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.

lessons.practice: Write a two-step shutdown routine for wig practice.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What small cleanup prevents a bigger problem?
lessons.readiness: You have a simple shutdown routine.

lessons.title: Cutting Is Permanent
lessons.concept: Synthetic wig cutting is unforgiving. Leave extra length, test the fall, then refine.

glossary.definition: The first conservative cut that removes length while leaving room to refine.
The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.

lessons.practice: Choose a pretend bang length, then write a safer longer first cut.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where would overcutting hurt most?
lessons.readiness: You know the first cut should leave room.

lessons.title: Section Before Cutting
lessons.concept: Sectioning keeps you from accidentally cutting too much or mixing display hair with support hair.

glossary.definition: Dividing hair into controlled areas before cutting, heating, teasing, or styling.
The visible hair layer that hides support work and gives the final finish.

lessons.practice: Describe one section you would clip away before trimming bangs.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What hair should not be cut yet?
lessons.readiness: You can name one protected section.

lessons.title: Face Frame
lessons.concept: Face-framing pieces shape the character and the wearer. They often matter more than back details in photos.

glossary.definition: The visible edge where wig hair begins around the forehead, temples, sideburns, or nape.
The outer shape of the hairstyle, usually the first thing viewers recognize.

lessons.practice: Look at one character and name the left and right face-frame shapes.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Are they symmetrical?
lessons.readiness: You can identify the face-frame pieces.

lessons.title: Point Cutting Idea
lessons.concept: Point cutting removes tiny notches from the end so a cut line looks less blocky. It still needs restraint.

glossary.definition: The first conservative cut that removes length while leaving room to refine.
Removing bulk without shortening the whole section, usually with thinning shears or careful cuts.

lessons.practice: Write when you might want a soft edge instead of a blunt edge.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which characters need graphic bluntness?
lessons.readiness: You know point cutting changes the edge feel.

lessons.title: Check On The Head
lessons.objective: Do not trust only the stand.
lessons.concept: Hair falls differently on a block than on a real head. Fit, height, and face shape can change the cut.

glossary.definition: The head form used for styling, cutting, fitting, and building wig structures.
How the wig or shell sits on the wearer's head without sliding, squeezing, or rubbing.

lessons.practice: Write one thing you would check while wearing the wig before final cutting.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What changes between block and head?
lessons.readiness: You can name one on-head check.

lessons.title: Layering Purpose
lessons.objective: Use layers for shape, not random thinning.
lessons.concept: Layering can remove bulk, create movement, or shape anime chunks. Each cut should have a job.

glossary.definition: Cutting hair at different lengths to remove bulk, create shape, or add movement.
The outer shape of the hairstyle, usually the first thing viewers recognize.

lessons.practice: Choose one reason to layer a wig: movement, volume control, or shape.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What happens if you layer without a goal?
lessons.readiness: You can state the purpose before cutting.

lessons.title: Thinning Risk
lessons.concept: Thinning removes bulk, but too much near the roots can reveal tracks or cap. Work gradually.

glossary.definition: Removing bulk without shortening the whole section, usually with thinning shears or careful cuts.
Visible wig cap or weft tracks showing through the hair.

lessons.practice: Name one area where thinning would be risky.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where is the cap easiest to expose?
lessons.readiness: You can identify a thinning danger zone.

lessons.title: Razor Texture Concept
lessons.concept: Razor texture can make ends feathered or anime-like, but it is not a fix for every wig.

glossary.definition: The visible hair layer that hides support work and gives the final finish.
The outer shape of the hairstyle, usually the first thing viewers recognize.

lessons.practice: Name one style that wants piecey texture.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Would this texture help realism or stylization?
lessons.readiness: You can say why texture is useful.

lessons.title: Before And After Photos
lessons.concept: Photos help you see changes that feel invisible while working. They also make mistakes easier to diagnose.

glossary.definition: A quick prompt that turns a task into a usable decision or lesson.
The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.

lessons.practice: Plan two photo angles before a cut: front and side.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which angle will reveal the problem fastest?
lessons.readiness: You have a tiny photo plan.

lessons.title: Stop Point
lessons.objective: Know when not to keep cutting.
lessons.concept: Many wig problems get worse when you chase perfection while tired. Define a stop point first.

glossary.definition: The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.
A quick prompt that turns a task into a usable decision or lesson.

lessons.practice: Write one sentence that tells you when to stop cutting for the day.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What is the sign you are overworking it?
lessons.readiness: You have a stop rule.

lessons.title: Crimping Creates Grip
lessons.concept: Crimping adds small waves that help hair hold shape and volume underneath a smoother surface.

glossary.definition: Adding tiny waves with a crimping iron to create grip, volume, and hidden structure.
The hidden underlayer that creates volume, grip, lift, or structure.

lessons.practice: Point to where crimping would hide under a visible layer.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Why not crimp the whole outside?
lessons.readiness: You can place crimping in a hidden layer.

lessons.title: Teasing Vs Backcombing
lessons.concept: Teasing and backcombing are closely related: both push fibers toward roots to create grip and volume.

glossary.definition: Backcombing fiber to create volume, texture, and internal grip.
Pushing fibers toward the roots to create hidden grip and volume.

lessons.practice: Write one place you would tease and one place you would keep smooth.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What should stay visible and clean?
lessons.readiness: You can separate support texture from surface finish.

lessons.title: Surface Layer
lessons.concept: The surface layer hides support work. It should look like the chosen character style, not the construction underneath.

glossary.definition: The visible hair layer that hides support work and gives the final finish.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Name one visible section that should stay smoother than the underlayer.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What construction might show through?
lessons.readiness: You can identify a display layer.

lessons.title: Small Volume Test
lessons.concept: Volume can come from crimping, teasing, added wefts, or support. Start with the lightest method that works.

glossary.definition: Structure under the visible surface that creates lift, shape, strength, or balance.
How much hair is present in an area; higher density looks fuller but adds weight.

lessons.practice: Rank crimping, teasing, and extra wefts from least to most material-heavy.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which method would you try first?
lessons.readiness: You can choose a low-commitment lift method.

lessons.title: Frizz Boundary
lessons.objective: Volume is not the same as mess.
lessons.concept: Support texture should not automatically make the surface frizzy. Clean outer layers matter.

glossary.definition: Frizz caused by rubbing against clothes, hands, collars, bags, or storage surfaces.
The visible hair layer that hides support work and gives the final finish.

lessons.practice: Write one way to protect the visible layer while building volume below.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where does useful texture become visual mess?
lessons.readiness: You can name one anti-frizz habit.

lessons.title: Product Is Not Structure
lessons.objective: Do not solve weight with spray.
lessons.concept: Spray and paste can finish a shape, but weight-bearing pieces need support, anchors, stitches, clips, or hardware.

glossary.definition: A bonding product; keep skin-safe adhesives separate from craft glues and styling sprays.
The route weight and force take through the wig into anchors and the wearer's head.

lessons.practice: Name one job for hairspray and one job it should not do.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What needs actual structure?
lessons.readiness: You can separate finish from support.

lessons.title: Freeze Hold Concept
lessons.concept: Strong spray can lock small spikes and flyaways, but it can become brittle if asked to do too much.

glossary.definition: A pointed or lifted hair shape that may need texture, product, heat, or support.
The visible hair layer that hides support work and gives the final finish.

lessons.practice: Choose one small surface detail that spray could control.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would break if it flexed?
lessons.readiness: You can name a cosmetic hold job.

lessons.title: Glue Categories
lessons.objective: Do not use one glue for everything.
lessons.concept: Craft glue, skin adhesive, and styling product solve different problems. Confusing them can damage skin or fail structurally.

glossary.definition: A bonding product; keep skin-safe adhesives separate from craft glues and styling sprays.
A product meant to bond something to skin and release with the correct remover.

lessons.practice: Sort these into skin or not-skin: spirit gum, hot glue, hairspray.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which one needs a remover?
lessons.readiness: You can separate skin adhesives from craft products.

lessons.title: Patch Test
lessons.concept: Anything that touches skin for wear should be patch tested when possible, especially adhesives and removers.

glossary.definition: A small skin test for adhesives, removers, or products before full use.
A product meant to bond something to skin and release with the correct remover.

lessons.practice: Write where and when you would patch test an adhesive.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would make you avoid using it?
lessons.readiness: You can explain the patch-test purpose.

lessons.title: Cleanup Plan
lessons.concept: Adhesive work is not planned until removal is planned. The correct remover is part of the tool list.

glossary.definition: A product meant to bond something to skin and release with the correct remover.
A short note replacing outside reading unless a lesson explicitly says otherwise.

lessons.practice: Write one adhesive and the matching remover you would verify before use.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What happens if removal is improvised?
lessons.readiness: You know remover planning is mandatory.

lessons.title: Anime Hair Is Graphic
lessons.concept: Drawn hair is graphic design. Reduce it into major chunks before deciding tools.

glossary.definition: The outer shape of the hairstyle, usually the first thing viewers recognize.
How quickly the hairstyle is recognizable as the intended character.

lessons.practice: Name the three biggest chunks in one character hairstyle.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which chunk matters most for recognition?
lessons.readiness: You can reduce a style to major forms.

lessons.title: Chunk Hierarchy
lessons.concept: Not every piece deserves equal effort. The biggest readable pieces should get the most planning.

glossary.definition: How quickly the hairstyle is recognizable as the intended character.
The empty shapes around hair chunks that help the silhouette read clearly.

lessons.practice: Rank three hair chunks as primary, secondary, or detail.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which piece could be simplified?
lessons.readiness: You can prioritize chunks.

lessons.title: Negative Space
lessons.concept: The spaces between hair chunks are often as recognizable as the chunks themselves.

glossary.definition: The empty shapes around hair chunks that help the silhouette read clearly.
The outer shape of the hairstyle, usually the first thing viewers recognize.

lessons.practice: Find one empty shape around a character's hair and describe it.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Would filling that gap ruin the read?
lessons.readiness: You can notice negative space.

lessons.title: Soft Spike
lessons.concept: Short spikes may only need cutting, heat direction, texture, and product. They are still tested, not guessed.

glossary.definition: A pointed or lifted hair shape that may need texture, product, heat, or support.
Shaping synthetic fiber with heat, steam, tension, clips, or rollers until it cools.

lessons.practice: Choose whether one spike is short enough to stay soft.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would make it need support?
lessons.readiness: You can classify a short spike.

lessons.title: Supported Spike
lessons.concept: Longer or heavier spikes need a support plan before surface styling. More spray is not the plan.

glossary.definition: Wire used to hold shape, spread load, or connect a piece to anchors.
A lightweight foam support used inside oversized spikes, buns, curls, or shapes.

lessons.practice: Pick wire or foam for a pretend long spike and say why.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which support is lighter or safer here?
lessons.readiness: You can choose a support type.

lessons.title: Shake Test
lessons.concept: A shake test reveals whether a spike, ponytail, or attachment is actually secure before final finishing.

glossary.definition: A movement test that checks whether a style or attachment survives motion.
The point or structure that carries pull, weight, clips, straps, or supports.

lessons.practice: Write what you would watch during a gentle shake test.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What failure would be acceptable in a mockup?
lessons.readiness: You can define a movement test.

lessons.title: Wefts Add More Than Hair
lessons.concept: Wefts can add density, color, coverage, repair material, and direction control. They also add weight.

glossary.definition: A sewn strip of wig hair that can be added, removed, moved, or harvested.
How much hair is present in an area; higher density looks fuller but adds weight.

lessons.practice: Name one reason to add a weft besides making hair fuller.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What problem could added weight create?
lessons.readiness: You can name two weft jobs.

lessons.title: Donor Wig Concept
lessons.concept: A donor wig provides matching or contrasting fiber for wefts, repairs, hairlines, or covering supports.

glossary.definition: A second wig used for extra fiber, wefts, color sections, or repairs.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Name one part of a build that could use donor wig fiber.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Does the fiber need to match exactly?
lessons.readiness: You can name one donor-wig use.

lessons.title: Weft Direction
lessons.concept: The direction a weft points affects how hair falls and what it covers. Placement is a design decision.

glossary.definition: The direction fibers travel so the style, seams, and coverage look intentional.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Draw one arrow showing how an added weft should fall.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would look wrong if it pointed the other way?
lessons.readiness: You can plan weft direction.

lessons.title: Reverse Weft Idea
lessons.concept: Reverse wefts help hair pull up or back without exposing tracks, especially for ponytails and updos.

glossary.definition: Wefting placed so hair can be pulled up or back without exposing tracks.
Visible wig cap or weft tracks showing through the hair.

lessons.practice: Name one style that might need reverse wefting.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where would tracks show?
lessons.readiness: You can identify a reverse-weft use case.

lessons.title: Tiny Sew Plan
lessons.concept: Even a small weft addition needs a start point, end point, direction, and knot/security plan.

glossary.definition: The point or structure that carries pull, weight, clips, straps, or supports.
A sewn strip of wig hair that can be added, removed, moved, or harvested.

lessons.practice: Write a one-line sew plan for a short hidden weft.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where should the thread start and stop?
lessons.readiness: You can describe a tiny sew plan.

lessons.title: Ponytail Weight
lessons.concept: Ponytails and pigtails pull on the cap. The higher or heavier they are, the more anchoring matters.

glossary.definition: The point or structure that carries pull, weight, clips, straps, or supports.
The route weight and force take through the wig into anchors and the wearer's head.

lessons.practice: Name where a ponytail's weight pulls on the wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would stop it sliding back?
lessons.readiness: You can identify the pull direction.

lessons.title: Clip-On Choice
lessons.concept: Clip-ons can reduce base-wig complexity, but they still need blending, balance, and secure placement.

glossary.definition: A separate hair piece attached with clips or combs instead of built into the base wig.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Pick one style where a clip-on would be easier than building in the ponytail.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What edge must be hidden?
lessons.readiness: You can decide if clip-on helps.

lessons.title: Braid Bulk
lessons.concept: Braids need more fiber than loose hair to look full. A thin braid can make a dense wig look strangely small.

glossary.definition: How much hair is present in an area; higher density looks fuller but adds weight.
A sewn strip of wig hair that can be added, removed, moved, or harvested.

lessons.practice: Estimate whether one character braid needs extra fiber.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Would a donor wig help?
lessons.readiness: You can spot braid-density needs.

lessons.title: Updo Exposure
lessons.concept: Any updo can reveal cap edges, tracks, lace, or sparse zones. Plan coverage before pulling hair back.

glossary.definition: Visible wig cap or weft tracks showing through the hair.
The visible edge where wig hair begins around the forehead, temples, sideburns, or nape.

lessons.practice: Name one area an updo might expose.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: How could you hide it?
lessons.readiness: You can name an updo exposure risk.

lessons.title: Anchor Backup
lessons.concept: Heavy attachments are safer when weight is spread or backed up. Single points fail more easily.

glossary.definition: The point or structure that carries pull, weight, clips, straps, or supports.
A physical attachment method like clips, screws, snaps, straps, buckles, or magnets.

lessons.practice: Name one backup for a heavy ponytail or bun.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What happens if the main anchor fails?
lessons.readiness: You can name a backup anchor idea.

lessons.title: Visible Edge Decision
lessons.concept: Not every visible edge needs high realism. Decide whether the hairline needs natural lace, stylized points, or simple hiding.

glossary.definition: A visible boundary such as hairline, lace, sideburn, nape, shell rim, or seam.
The visible edge where wig hair begins around the forehead, temples, sideburns, or nape.

lessons.practice: Choose one character edge and label it realistic, stylized, or hidden.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What does the viewer actually see?
lessons.readiness: You can classify an edge.

lessons.title: Ventilating Is Slow
lessons.concept: Ventilating ties individual hairs into lace or mesh. It is powerful but slow, so reserve it for visible edges and repairs.

glossary.definition: Tying individual hairs into lace or mesh to create hairlines, parts, or repairs.
A wig with sheer lace at the front where individual hairs create a realistic hairline.

lessons.practice: Name one place ventilating would be worth the time.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where would it be overkill?
lessons.readiness: You can name one high-value ventilating use.

lessons.title: Density Gradient
lessons.concept: A natural-looking hairline usually gets denser gradually. A sudden wall of hair can look fake.

glossary.definition: How much hair is present in an area; higher density looks fuller but adds weight.
The visible edge where wig hair begins around the forehead, temples, sideburns, or nape.

lessons.practice: Describe sparse-to-dense in one sentence.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where should the edge be lighter?
lessons.readiness: You can explain hairline density gradient.

lessons.title: False Hairline Idea
lessons.concept: A false hairline can use glued fibers, lace pieces, or styling to fake an edge without a full lace-front build.

glossary.definition: A visible boundary such as hairline, lace, sideburn, nape, shell rim, or seam.
A bonding product; keep skin-safe adhesives separate from craft glues and styling sprays.

lessons.practice: Name one style where a false hairline might be enough.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What makes it acceptable from viewing distance?
lessons.readiness: You can describe a false-edge option.

lessons.title: Sideburns And Nape
lessons.objective: Edges are not only the forehead.
lessons.concept: Temples, sideburns, and nape can expose wig construction when hair is short or pulled up.

glossary.definition: The side tab area of a wig near the ear, important for fit and edge blending.
A visible boundary such as hairline, lace, sideburn, nape, shell rim, or seam.

lessons.practice: Pick one non-forehead edge to check on a character.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Would movement reveal it?
lessons.readiness: You can name one side or back edge risk.

lessons.title: Hairline Decision Card
lessons.concept: A hairline decision card keeps the method simple: hide it, lace it, ventilate it, glue fibers, or stylize it.

glossary.definition: A simple plan that labels what each part of the wig must do.
The visible edge where wig hair begins around the forehead, temples, sideburns, or nape.

lessons.practice: Write one hairline method for one character.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Why that method instead of lace front by default?
lessons.readiness: You can choose a hairline method.

lessons.title: Color Reality Check
lessons.concept: Synthetic wigs usually cannot be lightened like human hair. Buying closer color is often safer than forcing dye.

glossary.definition: A small color or product test on scrap fiber before changing the whole wig.
The synthetic hair material the wig is made from.

lessons.practice: Write whether one color change is darker, lighter, or same-level.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Would buying a better base be easier?
lessons.readiness: You can identify risky color changes.

lessons.title: Swatch First
lessons.objective: Test color before the whole wig.
lessons.concept: A swatch test catches dye results, rub-off, texture change, and heat damage before the final wig is involved.

glossary.definition: A small color or product test on scrap fiber before changing the whole wig.
A hidden test on a small fiber section before using heat, dye, or product broadly.

lessons.practice: List three things a color swatch should check.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would make the swatch fail?
lessons.readiness: You can design a color swatch test.

lessons.title: Root Shadow
lessons.concept: A darker root can add depth or match a character without recoloring the entire wig.

glossary.definition: The direction fibers travel so the style, seams, and coverage look intentional.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Name one character or style where darker roots would help.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Would it improve realism or accuracy?
lessons.readiness: You can identify a root-shadow use.

lessons.title: Rub-Off Risk
lessons.concept: Sprays, markers, and dyes can transfer to skin, collars, props, or hands. Test contact, not just color.

glossary.definition: A small color or product test on scrap fiber before changing the whole wig.
Frizz caused by rubbing against clothes, hands, collars, bags, or storage surfaces.

lessons.practice: Name one surface the wig might rub against.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would be ruined by color transfer?
lessons.readiness: You can name one rub-off risk.

lessons.title: Buy Vs Modify
lessons.concept: Sometimes the best color technique is buying a closer wig or adding a colored weft, not dyeing.

glossary.definition: A second wig used for extra fiber, wefts, color sections, or repairs.
A sewn strip of wig hair that can be added, removed, moved, or harvested.

lessons.practice: Choose buy, add weft, or color-test for one pretend color problem.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which option risks the final wig least?
lessons.readiness: You can choose a safer color path.

lessons.title: Hard Cap Purpose
lessons.concept: A hard cap carries shapes that hair alone cannot hold. It must fit, breathe, and avoid sharp edges.

glossary.definition: A rigid or semi-rigid wig base built to hold sculptural shapes.
A rigid or semi-rigid base structure that carries a shape, usually hidden under hair.

lessons.practice: Name one hairstyle that might justify a hard cap.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What comfort issue would you check first?
lessons.readiness: You can explain why a shell might exist.

lessons.title: Shell Mockup
lessons.concept: A paper, tape, or scrap foam mockup can check size and shape before final materials.

glossary.definition: A quick test version made before committing to final materials.
A rigid or semi-rigid base structure that carries a shape, usually hidden under hair.

lessons.practice: Describe a mini mockup for one large hair shape.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What question would the mockup answer?
lessons.readiness: You can name one mockup goal.

lessons.title: Armature Choice
lessons.objective: Pick a hidden skeleton only when needed.
lessons.concept: An armature can hold shape, but it also adds complexity, weight, and safety concerns.

glossary.definition: A hidden support skeleton, often wire, foam, mesh, or plastic, that holds a shape.
The route weight and force take through the wig into anchors and the wearer's head.

lessons.practice: Choose whether a floating ahoge needs no support, wire, or foam.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What carries the load?
lessons.readiness: You can choose or reject armature support.

lessons.title: Foam Core Coverage
lessons.concept: Foam can make large shapes lighter than solid hair, but it must be covered convincingly.

glossary.definition: A lightweight foam support used inside oversized spikes, buns, curls, or shapes.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Name how you would cover one foam shape: wefts, fiber sheet, or surface hair.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where might foam show?
lessons.readiness: You can pair foam with a coverage method.

lessons.title: Wire Safety
lessons.concept: Wire can support shapes, but ends and pressure points must be protected from the wearer and the wig fiber.

glossary.definition: Wire used to hold shape, spread load, or connect a piece to anchors.
A spot where weight, edge, clip, strap, or shell presses uncomfortably.

lessons.practice: Write one safety rule for wire inside a wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Where could wire poke or bend badly?
lessons.readiness: You can name one wire safety risk.

lessons.title: Mechanical Attachments
lessons.objective: Use hardware when glue is not enough.
lessons.concept: Large headpieces often need clips, screws, snaps, straps, magnets, or other mechanical support.

glossary.definition: A physical attachment method like clips, screws, snaps, straps, buckles, or magnets.
The point or structure that carries pull, weight, clips, straps, or supports.

lessons.practice: Pick one attachment method for horns, crown, or detachable hair mass.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Why is glue alone weak here?
lessons.readiness: You can choose one mechanical method.

lessons.title: Detachable Sections
lessons.concept: A detachable section can make transport, repair, and wearing easier if the connection is planned from the start.

glossary.definition: A physical attachment method like clips, screws, snaps, straps, buckles, or magnets.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Name one hair piece that should detach for packing.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: How would you hide the join?
lessons.readiness: You can name one detachable build option.

lessons.title: Ventilation Gap
lessons.concept: Rigid or dense builds need some comfort planning. Airflow and heat buildup matter before final finish.

glossary.definition: A timed trial wear to check heat, pressure, slipping, visibility, comfort, and failure points.
A wig built over a rigid or semi-rigid shell, usually for large sculptural hair.

lessons.practice: Name one place airflow could exist without ruining the look.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would make the wig too hot?
lessons.readiness: You can name one ventilation idea.

lessons.title: Removal Method
lessons.objective: Plan how the wig comes off.
lessons.concept: If a wig uses adhesive, hardware, or a shell, removal must be safe and reachable.

glossary.definition: A product meant to bond something to skin and release with the correct remover.
A physical attachment method like clips, screws, snaps, straps, buckles, or magnets.

lessons.practice: Write one removal step for a complex wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What should not require panic or force?
lessons.readiness: You can name a safe removal step.

lessons.title: Transport Shape
lessons.concept: A helmet wig, spike wig, and loose wig need different protection. Packing is part of the design.

glossary.definition: A wig built over a rigid or semi-rigid shell, usually for large sculptural hair.
Products and tools packed for quick fixes during wear.

lessons.practice: Choose box, stand, bag, or custom support for one style.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What would break in transit?
lessons.readiness: You can choose transport based on shape.

lessons.title: Milestone: Soft Wig Plan
lessons.concept: A soft wig project should use a base wig, small cut, simple shaping, and a clear stop point.

glossary.definition: The starting wig chosen for color, length, cap type, density, and hairline needs.
The first conservative cut that removes length while leaving room to refine.

lessons.practice: Write a three-step plan for a short or medium soft wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What makes it manageable?
lessons.readiness: You have a tiny soft-wig plan.

lessons.title: Milestone: Support Sample
lessons.concept: Before a huge build, prove one support idea in miniature: spike, bun, ahoge, ponytail, or horn integration.

glossary.definition: A quick test version made before committing to final materials.
A movement test that checks whether a style or attachment survives motion.

lessons.practice: Pick one support sample and write what it must prove.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What failure would teach you the most?
lessons.readiness: You have one structural sample idea.

lessons.title: Milestone: Edge Sample
lessons.concept: A hairline or sideburn test is smaller than a full wig and reveals whether the method looks acceptable.

glossary.definition: A visible boundary such as hairline, lace, sideburn, nape, shell rim, or seam.
Tying individual hairs into lace or mesh to create hairlines, parts, or repairs.

lessons.practice: Choose one edge sample: lace, false hairline, sideburn, or nape.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: What viewing distance matters?
lessons.readiness: You have one edge sample idea.

lessons.title: Photo Checklist
lessons.concept: Good photos show front, side, back, three-quarter, edge, and any interior support. They make critique specific.

glossary.definition: How closely the wig matches the character reference while still working on a real head.
How well hair, fiber sheets, lace, or panels hide supports, gaps, and construction.

lessons.practice: Write three photos you would take of a finished wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Which photo would reveal the biggest lie?
lessons.readiness: You have a three-shot checklist.

lessons.title: Critique With Causes
lessons.concept: Useful critique says why something looks wrong: fit, density, edge, silhouette, color, coverage, or support.

glossary.definition: A quick prompt that turns a task into a usable decision or lesson.
How closely the wig matches the character reference while still working on a real head.

lessons.practice: Pick one possible flaw and name its likely cause.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Is the problem visual, structural, or comfort-related?
lessons.readiness: You can critique by cause.

lessons.title: Next Skill Target
lessons.concept: A course should end with a next practice target, not a vague feeling of being done.

glossary.definition: The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.
A quick prompt that turns a task into a usable decision or lesson.

lessons.practice: Choose one skill to practice on scraps before using an expensive wig.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.
lessons.reflection: Why that skill first?
lessons.readiness: You have one next practice target.

lessons.title: Personal Workflow
lessons.objective: Turn lessons into a repeatable order.
lessons.concept: A simple workflow prevents starting with the fun step and skipping fit, tests, and support planning.

glossary.definition: A simple plan that labels what each part of the wig must do.
The specific proof that today's tiny lesson is understood enough to move on.

lessons.practice: Write a five-step personal wig workflow from reference to storage.
lessons.safety: Stop before heat, blades, adhesives, dye, or dust if the setup is not ventilated, protected, and tested on scrap first.

lessons.title: Course Review
lessons.concept: The useful version of this course is one tiny lesson per day, self-contained unless you choose to deep dive.

glossary.definition: A short note replacing outside reading unless a lesson explicitly says otherwise.
A quick prompt that turns a task into a usable decision or lesson.

lessons.practice: Write what lesson type was most useful: word, decision, sketch, test, or checklist.
